claim: He’s building Vercel into an AI cloud for the world of agents and whatever comes after that.
speaker: Nivi

claim: He’s building Boom Supersonic—supersonic aircraft, in his own factory, and jet engines as well.
speaker: Nivi

claim: I can’t remember my exact quote, but I’ve been really pilled with this idea of software factories.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
The job of the engineer being something where you just show up to work, you ship the output directly, and everything inside the company was—“how good is person A at shipping output B?”

And now what’s happening is, the way I’m judging you as an engineer is, “are you producing the factory that will produce multiplicative outputs B through Z?”

That’s a pretty significant change.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We used to believe—and it used to be somewhat controversial—that there are 10x engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Now clearly there’s 100x or 1,000x engineers, and the world hasn’t fully adjusted to this.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I used to get flamed on Twitter for saying there are 10x engineers, because it flies in the face of so much equality philosophy that everyone’s equal.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But the reality is, when you’re operating in idea domains, in intellectual and virtual digital domains, it’s not even 10x—it’s 100x or 1,000x, and it always has been.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Not to even mention—if you choose the right thing to work on versus the wrong thing to work on, that’s an infinity difference.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And it could just be not necessarily a better programmer, just one who had better judgment on what to work on in the first place.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And now obviously it’s less controversial because of AI leverage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: What’s controversial is the token leaderboards.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: People are still getting a little confused—“Well, I have a bunch of 100x engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
Look at all these tokens that I’m paying for.”

I’m curious if you guys have seen the same—how do you measure ROI?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But at this stage, it really seems to reflect back the judgment that the user brings in.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I’ll throw Codex, Claude, and Gemini at the same problem over and over and just waste tokens to save time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Even if they’re writing low-quality code—which I know in many cases they are—when the time comes and I want to ship to production, I’ll just throw more tokens at it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: As long as we have verifiable domains and solved problems, they’re going to resolve those problems.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now in the unsolved problems domain—maybe you’re Terence Tao, at the cutting edge of creativity—you need to be working very collaboratively and carefully and closely with the model.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you’re a really proficient engineer or architect, you’re still extracting more juice.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Clearly not, because a junior gets more advanced knowledge in code than they would have been able to write by themselves.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But doesn’t an experienced architect get 10x where a junior engineer gets 2x?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I’m seeing this now with some of our junior software engineers on the team—what’s the next step in their career progression?
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s going from writing implementation for a feature to picking technologies.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Choosing between Postgres versus some other database.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They’ll give you a really good trade-offs matrix.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Pretty soon every good SaaS company or hosting provider will have a CLI and API interface the models can use directly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: As long as it’s text-based, Unix-based—the agent can hack its own API.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And the money part—you insert crypto tokens, put in Bitcoin, put in whatever, and the model goes and pays for whatever it needs.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The thing I’m now thinking through is—is pure software dead?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Is pure software engineering an obsolete thing?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We had to learn code to communicate with them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You had to build hardware, and it was hard to build a software company alongside.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Patrick Collison says, “Software is art, and it’s hard to hire artists.”

Now, as a hardware founder—great, you can have really good software developed fairly quickly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you’re creating models, maybe that’s the new software engineering—training, tweaking, post-training, fine-tuning.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But classic software engineering—is that dead?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Is pure software something you can organize a company and a team around, and try to get some leverage?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Did you guys see—there was an article on X by Mitchell Hashimoto called “The Building Block Economy”?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: His argument is that the most useful thing for agents to have now is really powerful reusable building blocks.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To Max’s example, you wouldn’t expect your clanker to reinvent a queue infrastructure system every time it needs to send an email.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
It needs to bring in the right building block, right-sized for the task—“Okay, for this one it’s BullMQ.”

I challenge the notion that I’d want the agent to reinvent the entire universe from first principles in a way that’s incompatible with the rest of society and civilization.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s almost like reinventing highways, laws, policies—just for you.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
Even if there’s potential for extra optimization, there’s still cooperation-at-large-scale value of saying “we’re both depending on Postgres 13.2.”

The category of infrastructure software and building blocks these agents are going to use is—obviously in bias, this is what we’re building—extremely valuable.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I don’t see the agent reinventing all of that any time soon.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Another metaphor I’ve been using: anything that’s already been created that the models can reuse is like a token cache.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s going to change things quite profoundly.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Anyone who understands what an API is, how data flows, inputs and outputs, performance—because you have to orient the model around “this is the level of expectation I have out of this operation.” That has always been infinitely more useful than writing code.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: A really proficient engineering leader has been quote-unquote vibe coding through people on Slack or one-on-ones—you’re transmitting your will, your intent, your experience, and letting others run with it.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I went from not having written code in twenty years to coding all the time now—through agents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It turns out that just understanding the basic principles of software engineering and algorithms gets you a long way.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The reason I stopped coding was that I didn’t have time to figure out the latest language, the latest architecture, the infrastructure pieces to plug into.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The thing that really changed is—it used to be you could build a lot, a lot would go straightforward, but then you’d hit some random thing and you could spend an indefinite period of time debugging some narrow thing.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Now, with agents, you just don’t get stuck anymore.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It completely changes the role of software and hardware developers.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: From day one we tried to take a lot of traditional engineering workflows—hardware engineering workflows—and turn them into software.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you haven’t been around hardware engineering, let me try to make this clear.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: A lot of hardware engineering happens in Excel spreadsheets on engineers’ laptops in a silo.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Very complex spreadsheets, sometimes with VBScript code.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: All of this is actually software, but it’s treated as if it’s not.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There’s no source control, no automated testing.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you want to hand something off from an aerodynamicist to a structures engineer, that’s done manually with a spreadsheet over email.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: So we started building software frameworks to automate and make repeatable hardware engineering flows, with the idea that we could reduce the cost of iteration.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: But it was slow going—we could never afford enough software engineers.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What we’ve gotten into now is a mind-blowingly different model: the software engineers create the architectures, because they understand systems, algorithms, and division of concerns.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Then the hardware engineers can vibe-code their pieces because they know hardware engineering.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The result is mind-blowingly different productivity for small teams.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you’re designing a turbine blade—classically, a turbine blade starts cold, but when it runs it gets hot, so it gets bigger.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: You have to convert between cold and hot, between structures and aerodynamics.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: This takes one engineer one day for one blade for one piece of the analysis.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There are about a thousand blades in a jet engine.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Now, with a combination of software and hardware people creating the solution, you can change blade geometry and see in real time the structures and aerodynamics results.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Two engineers can design an entire jet engine.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: One of the things you mentioned is that software engineers are creating the tools and architectures for the rest of the engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To me, that’s the biggest cataclysm of enterprise software—there’s no startup that builds hardware collaboration tools that can sell you anything anymore.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Internally, you’re just coding the right thing you need at any given time.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The thing AI hasn’t come to yet, but I think it will within the next year—probably within 2026—and that will be very exciting: right now it can generate software, but soon it will generate STEP files and PCB layouts.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: When it comes for mechanical and electrical engineering, that’s a whole other thing we haven’t seen yet.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: On the hardware side, this is a boon for all these little gadget companies and part companies that write really bad software because they can’t make great software.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now they’re going to be able to make good-enough software.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Or it may not even be software with a human front end—it might just be completely agentic, an agent accessing it, and you talk to it through voice to control hardware.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re going all in on it because they have hardware superiority.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They have these very complex supply chains and component chains.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
They’re basically saying—“hey, if I can just generate software on demand, then I don’t have this disadvantage anymore against Silicon Valley.”

That’s not the only reason they’re doing open source.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re also behind, they’re distilling models, they’re catching up, they’re collaborating on resources.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They want to pool all their resources, catch up on AI, and use it to give their hardware stuff an advantage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It helps our hardware founders, but it helps their hardware founders and factories that much more.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: All the crappy little software that goes with all the random knickknacks and thingamajigs you buy off Amazon to tinker with on a lazy Saturday afternoon—that software’s getting a lot better very quickly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s not just about producing software—in any piece of this hardware pipeline, like Blake was saying, you need to generate software.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: If you fall behind in your ability to generate software, you fall behind in your ability to generate everything.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: And it’s always cheaper than a real person, and real-time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Given the amount of leverage I’m going to pour into it—through capital and code and people and marketing—I want to make the right decision every time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We have AI Gateway data—basically every application agent goes through it.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: For a lot of tasks other than coding, interestingly enough, they’re the best industrial production models.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But any time I’m working to push the frontier, you need the best possible coding model.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: In order to do that type of integration—to actually innovate beyond just piecing together things you can buy off the shelf, which is really very limiting—you have to learn to do it yourself.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: So we own a captive MEMS foundry on the East Coast.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We’ve instrumented our foundry as well as many other parts of the company in ways where, as these models get better, that should show up pretty immediately in things like the cell engineering we’re doing and the material science we’re developing.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: For junior engineers and junior engineering—junior engineers basically got a promotion to senior engineer, and junior engineering got taken over by agents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s actually interesting to think about the parallels between how software engineering is evolving and lawyers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You never know exactly what lawyers put into these documents—you just trust them.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Can you do red lines?” What you’re valuing in the relationship with a lawyer is that they’re a trusted authority.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s a parallel with software engineering.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There are all these memes on Twitter—“way back in the day we used to read every line of code of a PR.” Well, in my world—infrastructure—I want engineers to be able to say “I understand” every line of that PR.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
I’m signing off on understanding the consequences.” Or, “I wrote the test harness, the simulations, the proofs, the type-checkers—even without reading this, I have confidence I can sign off that it’ll be safe in production.”

There’s a world in which we embrace that everything is going to be spaghetti code we don’t fully understand, but we write the evaluators that give us confidence, and we rely on people—the infrastructure production engineers—to say, “Okay, I’m fine sending this into prod.” Someone is going to get paged if your systems go down.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Another thing people are underestimating: creating software is really easy, zero to one.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: And are you still motivated to invest all those tokens in maintaining it in prod?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: That’s how we train these models—with good verification data—and now we need human verifiers.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: A lot of the old function of people, lawyers, engineers, operations people, moves to verifying the stack and saying, “Yeah, this is roughly correct, I’ll roughly stand behind it, I’ll support you if it goes wrong.”
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: One of the things we’ve seen related to regulatory—it massively reduces change aversion and improves iteration.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Example: let’s say you’re going to certify an airplane.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: One of the zillions of things you have to do is prove it can withstand a lightning strike.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The regulatory documentation for the test plan stretches on for, say, 200 pages.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What you would classically do is hire a—let’s be honest—not super-bright engineer who’s willing to be there, monkey at keyboard, writing 200 pages of regulatory compliance documentation.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What we’ve found is we can build a RAG that will enable us to basically prompt our way through all of that work in—let’s call it minutes.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The first-order effect is you save a lot of time.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: And the third-order effect is you can get rid of the not-very-great engineers and have a small number of really creative ones who can iterate rapidly, because the cost of change goes down.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: In a certain sense, the entire regulatory burden—which really hurts the ability to iterate—drops away.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The consensus in Silicon Valley is that regulation sucks—we want to go faster, we want to realize this amazing future, we want abundance, prosperity, and stuff that slows down that future is to be avoided.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But a lot of the regulations themselves are not the problem.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The problem is that it’s really difficult for humans to deal with understanding and complying with this, and every time you have to exchange a letter with the government, you wait months.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you could take a lot of the things we’ve learned and make them totally frictionless, that would be pretty cool.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: When Max was talking about this potentially being a good thing—that we have all this regulation—my head went to: the thing that makes agents successful is humans or other agents setting up the right testing guardrails.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Your exit criteria is that you’ve complied with all of these regulations.” There’s totally a world where we say the regulations are great—they’re like our test suite.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It creates an opportunity to really shift the regulatory model.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Before you could go anywhere, you’d have to write a plan, ship it to some regulator, and wait.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: We were responsible, we did clinical trials, we reported all the data, but—
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: When they start regulating frontier models and start regulating GPUs, that stops as well.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Well, it’s been held back by huge regulatory barriers.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You can always find a scary case—a vaccine, or a famous medical disaster—but the regulations spread everywhere, the tentacles are everywhere, and there are all these contradictory regulatory bodies.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: SpaceX got sued for not having enough—I forget what—migrants or refugees or whatever, but they’re not allowed to hire them, by government regulation on the other side, because they’re not citizens.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: This is not like logical code that has to compile in one place.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: These are made-up random regulations all over the place.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You might comply with one state and violate another, violate federal over here, annoy this guy over here, that guy chooses to prosecute one out of fifty people who are his friend.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I want to be really clear—I’m on the side of deregulation on a lot of this.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I think that is the most important problem to solve in the regulatory state.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: They’ve got these notified bodies—basically private businesses blessed by their host governments to certify things.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They themselves have to be compliant with conditions placed by host governments, but it means there can be many thousands more reviewers than in the US.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But generally, if you don’t have some private market where people are paying out of pocket for what are medical procedures, you’re just not going to get this feedback loop.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We have a capability that fires off upon finding anomalies—I recommend everyone create a version of this, or Vercel offers one.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We’re doing everything except giving the agent the tools to change prod—we’re serving solutions on a silver platter to engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s incredible—like Mythos, but you get it today.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We run it against our entire monorepo using ten thousand concurrent agents in the cloud.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It found several quarters’ worth of security-research progress in a couple of days, for fourteen thousand dollars of tokens—months of red-teaming, entire teams of people.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We got the opposite—a large number of needle-movers and very few silly projects.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: But if they can go from idea to an actual thing, they can react and iterate.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Give them a week, and by the end they’ve built something that makes sense.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There’s a culture change happening: a lot of people coming in intuitively know their job isn’t to work on the thing, it’s to train the agent that works on the thing.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: That’s hard to see in the job-career mindset, because you hire people to do the same thing over and over, and that’s going away.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I think it’s 99% intelligence and 1% agency—because the agents will exercise the agency.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’ll literally say, “Hey agent, I’m making smart decisions and thinking big thoughts; just go implement stuff.” Sometimes I want to build a feature on an app I’m vibe-coding, and I’ll ask the agent, “What feature should I build next?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We all know a lot of people now who are coding who weren’t before—including, in many cases, ourselves.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The percentage of coders has probably gone up 10x.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So we might’ve gone from 0.01% of the population writing code to maybe 1%—call it 100x—but 99% still never will.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But video has its own issues—someday “make me a great movie about X” spits out a good documentary, but right now they don’t have the taste or the judgment.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Humans are the ones who generate surprise completely out of the data distribution, and they do it with intent—and intent matters for meaning.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Maybe AI is starting to do that at the edges, as we’re seeing with some math problems—but math is a very bounded domain.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Human without AI, forget it; pure AI isn’t there yet—but human plus AI, we’re in that era, and I’m betting we stay there longer than people think.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Everyone here, our productivity has gone through the roof.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Basic economics says that when productivity is higher, you’re wealthier and you hire more people, not fewer.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If someone’s really good with AI and really smart and creative, I want to hire them more than ever, for the leverage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We’re hiring juniors and super-seniors, as long as they’re really good with agents and quick to adapt.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: My hypothesis is we end up with a larger number of smaller teams.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The number of people required for any given task drops a lot.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
People who only see first-order effects say, “All the jobs disappear—I can do a jet engine with two people, not a thousand; 998 jobs gone.”

But what it actually means is you can create a lot of different jet engines.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We’ll get an explosion of entrepreneurship, an explosion of founders, and a very large number of very small teams.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: AI provided base-level intelligence and domain knowledge and cut through the jargon; now agents provide a lot of agency.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So what’s left is creativity, taste—and yes, you need enough agency to get started and to stick with it, but you don’t need to spend twenty years learning one thing before you can contribute.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: That barrier going down means generalists are having a field day.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: At the end of the day we’re all generalists—we like to think about everything.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Max is here talking about consciousness and the FDA and brain science and creativity.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The people on Twitter fond of saying “experts, credentials, sources” are the ones getting hurt, because the expertise matters less now.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You spend five or ten years getting a PhD—hopefully it developed your creativity, instincts, taste, and judgment, because if all it did was help you memorize jargon and scaffolding, AI cuts right through that.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So it’s people with AI versus people without AI—and the single best thing you can do for yourself is get really good with these tools, and always know the edges of what they can and can’t do.

claim: I just try to figure things out, and then if there’s something I believe in, I try to convey that as accurately and honestly as possible to the other person.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Because I think deep, deep, deep down, this is what every person really craves, which is if you’re building something, you’re in that kind of world where you want to be with a small team of highly competent people, each pulling their weight and doing their job to the best, such that you can completely rely on them, and then you get to do your creativity at the max.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you see low trust societies, where people don’t trust each other, or where the structure makes it impossible to work together—the taxes are too high, the regulations are too high, there’s too much bureaucracy—things are illegal.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You want to function with high-trust people in small groups to solve really hard problems.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So if you’re going to give that up, it only makes sense to do that for something that has that opportunity to be 100x or 1,000x on your time or on your capital.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But if you feel like, “I can walk away from this and I can recover my time and I know not to work with these people again,” or “I know that these stakes are too small to bother fighting over,” then you just don’t do it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It completely changes the role of software and hardware developers.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: From day one we tried to take a lot of traditional engineering workflows—hardware engineering workflows—and turn them into software.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you haven’t been around hardware engineering, let me try to make this clear.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: A lot of hardware engineering happens in Excel spreadsheets on engineers’ laptops in a silo.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Very complex spreadsheets, sometimes with VBScript code.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: All of this is actually software, but it’s treated as if it’s not.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There’s no source control, no automated testing.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you want to hand something off from an aerodynamicist to a structures engineer, that’s done manually with a spreadsheet over email.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: So we started building software frameworks to automate and make repeatable hardware engineering flows, with the idea that we could reduce the cost of iteration.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: But it was slow going—we could never afford enough software engineers.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What we’ve gotten into now is a mind-blowingly different model: the software engineers create the architectures, because they understand systems, algorithms, and division of concerns.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Then the hardware engineers can vibe-code their pieces because they know hardware engineering.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The result is mind-blowingly different productivity for small teams.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you’re designing a turbine blade—classically, a turbine blade starts cold, but when it runs it gets hot, so it gets bigger.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: You have to convert between cold and hot, between structures and aerodynamics.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: This takes one engineer one day for one blade for one piece of the analysis.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There are about a thousand blades in a jet engine.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Now, with a combination of software and hardware people creating the solution, you can change blade geometry and see in real time the structures and aerodynamics results.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Two engineers can design an entire jet engine.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: One of the things you mentioned is that software engineers are creating the tools and architectures for the rest of the engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To me, that’s the biggest cataclysm of enterprise software—there’s no startup that builds hardware collaboration tools that can sell you anything anymore.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Internally, you’re just coding the right thing you need at any given time.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The thing AI hasn’t come to yet, but I think it will within the next year—probably within 2026—and that will be very exciting: right now it can generate software, but soon it will generate STEP files and PCB layouts.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: When it comes for mechanical and electrical engineering, that’s a whole other thing we haven’t seen yet.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: On the hardware side, this is a boon for all these little gadget companies and part companies that write really bad software because they can’t make great software.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now they’re going to be able to make good-enough software.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Or it may not even be software with a human front end—it might just be completely agentic, an agent accessing it, and you talk to it through voice to control hardware.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re going all in on it because they have hardware superiority.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They have these very complex supply chains and component chains.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re basically saying—“hey, if I can just generate software on demand, then I don’t have this disadvantage anymore against Silicon Valley.” That’s not the only reason they’re doing open source.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re also behind, they’re distilling models, they’re catching up, they’re collaborating on resources.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They want to pool all their resources, catch up on AI, and use it to give their hardware stuff an advantage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It helps our hardware founders, but it helps their hardware founders and factories that much more.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: All the crappy little software that goes with all the random knickknacks and thingamajigs you buy off Amazon to tinker with on a lazy Saturday afternoon—that software’s getting a lot better very quickly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s not just about producing software—in any piece of this hardware pipeline, like Blake was saying, you need to generate software.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: If you fall behind in your ability to generate software, you fall behind in your ability to generate everything.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: And it’s always cheaper than a real person, and real-time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Given the amount of leverage I’m going to pour into it—through capital and code and people and marketing—I want to make the right decision every time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We have AI Gateway data—basically every application agent goes through it.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: For a lot of tasks other than coding, interestingly enough, they’re the best models.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But any time I’m working to push the frontier, you need the best possible coding model.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: In order to do that type of integration—to actually innovate beyond just piecing together things you can buy off the shelf, which is really very limiting—you have to learn to do it yourself.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: So we own a captive MEMS foundry on the East Coast.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We’ve instrumented our foundry as well as many other parts of the company in ways where, as these models get better, that should show up pretty immediately in things like the cell engineering we’re doing and the material science we’re developing.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: For junior engineers and junior engineering—junior engineers basically got a promotion to senior engineer, and junior engineering got taken over by agents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s actually interesting to think about the parallels between how software engineering is evolving and lawyers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You never know exactly what lawyers put into these documents—you just trust them.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Can you do red lines?” What you’re valuing in the relationship with a lawyer is that they’re a trusted authority.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s a parallel with software engineering.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There are all these memes on Twitter—“way back in the day we used to read every line of code of a PR.” Well, in my world—infrastructure—I want engineers to be able to say “I understand” every line of that PR.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
I’m signing off on understanding the consequences.” Or, “I wrote the test harness, the simulations, the proofs, the type-checkers—even without reading this, I have confidence I can sign off that it’ll be safe in production.”

There’s a world in which we embrace that everything is going to be spaghetti code we don’t fully understand, but we write the evaluators that give us confidence, and we rely on people—the infrastructure production engineers—to say, “Okay, I’m fine sending this into prod.” Someone is going to get paged if your systems go down.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Another thing people are underestimating: creating software is really easy, zero to one.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: And are you still motivated to invest all those tokens in maintaining it in prod?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: That’s how we train these models—with good verification data—and now we need human verifiers.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: A lot of the old function of people, lawyers, engineers, operations people, moves to verifying the stack and saying, “Yeah, this is roughly correct, I’ll roughly stand behind it, I’ll support you if it goes wrong.”
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: He’s building Vercel into an AI cloud for the world of agents and whatever comes after that.
speaker: Nivi

claim: He’s building Boom Supersonic—supersonic aircraft, in his own factory, and jet engines as well.
speaker: Nivi

claim: I can’t remember my exact quote, but I’ve been really pilled with this idea of software factories.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The job of the engineer being something where you just show up to work, you ship the output directly, and everything inside the company was—”how good is person A at shipping output B?” And now what’s happening is, the way I’m judging you as an engineer is, “are you producing the factory that will produce multiplicative outputs B through Z?” That’s a pretty significant change.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We used to believe—and it used to be somewhat controversial—that there are 10x engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Now clearly there’s 100x or 1,000x engineers, and the world hasn’t fully adjusted to this.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I used to get flamed on Twitter for saying there are 10x engineers, because it flies in the face of so much equality philosophy that everyone’s equal.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But the reality is, when you’re operating in idea domains, in intellectual and virtual digital domains, it’s not even 10x—it’s 100x or 1,000x, and it always has been.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Not to even mention—if you choose the right thing to work on versus the wrong thing to work on, that’s an infinity difference.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And it could just be not necessarily a better programmer, just one who had better judgment on what to work on in the first place.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And now obviously it’s less controversial because of AI leverage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: What’s controversial is the token leaderboards.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: People are still getting a little confused—”Well, I have a bunch of 100x engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Look at all these tokens that I’m paying for.” I’m curious if you guys have seen the same—how do you measure ROI?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But at this stage, it really seems to reflect back the judgment that the user brings in.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I’ll throw Codex, Claude, and Gemini at the same problem over and over and just waste tokens to save time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Even if they’re writing low-quality code—which I know in many cases they are—when the time comes and I want to ship to production, I’ll just throw more tokens at it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: As long as we have verifiable domains and solved problems, they’re going to resolve those problems.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now in the unsolved problems domain—maybe you’re Terence Tao, at the cutting edge of creativity—you need to be working very collaboratively and carefully and closely with the model.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you’re a really proficient engineer or architect, you’re still extracting more juice.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But doesn’t an experienced architect get 10x where a junior engineer gets 2x?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I’m seeing this now with some of our junior software engineers on the team—what’s the next step in their career progression?
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s going from writing implementation for a feature to picking technologies.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Choosing between Postgres versus some other database.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They’ll give you a really good trade-offs matrix.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Pretty soon every good SaaS company or hosting provider will have a CLI and API interface the models can use directly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: As long as it’s text-based, Unix-based—the agent can hack its own API.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And the money part—you insert crypto tokens, put in Bitcoin, put in whatever, and the model goes and pays for whatever it needs.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The thing I’m now thinking through is—is pure software dead?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Is pure software engineering an obsolete thing?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We had to learn code to communicate with them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You had to build hardware, and it was hard to build a software company alongside.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Patrick Collison says, “Software is art, and it’s hard to hire artists.”

Now, as a hardware founder—great, you can have really good software developed fairly quickly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you’re creating models, maybe that’s the new software engineering—training, tweaking, post-training, fine-tuning.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But classic software engineering—is that dead?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Is pure software something you can organize a company and a team around, and try to get some leverage?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Did you guys see—there was an article on X by Mitchell Hashimoto called “The Building Block Economy”?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: His argument is that the most useful thing for agents to have now is really powerful reusable building blocks.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To Max’s example, you wouldn’t expect your clanker to reinvent a queue infrastructure system every time it needs to send an email.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
It needs to bring in the right building block, right-sized for the task—”Okay, for this one it’s BullMQ.”

I challenge the notion that I’d want the agent to reinvent the entire universe from first principles in a way that’s incompatible with the rest of society and civilization.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s almost like reinventing highways, laws, policies—just for you.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
Even if there’s potential for extra optimization, there’s still cooperation-at-large-scale value of saying “we’re both depending on Postgres 13.2.”

The category of infrastructure software and building blocks these agents are going to use is—obviously in bias, this is what we’re building—extremely valuable.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I don’t see the agent reinventing all of that any time soon.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Another metaphor I’ve been using: anything that’s already been created that the models can reuse is like a token cache.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s going to change things quite profoundly.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Anyone who understands what an API is, how data flows, inputs and outputs, performance—because you have to orient the model around “this is the level of expectation I have out of this operation.” That has always been infinitely more useful than writing code.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: A really proficient engineering leader has been quote-unquote vibe coding through people on Slack or one-on-ones—you’re transmitting your will, your intent, your experience, and letting others run with it.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I went from not having written code in twenty years to coding all the time now—through agents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It turns out that just understanding the basic principles of software engineering and algorithms gets you a long way.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The reason I stopped coding was that I didn’t have time to figure out the latest language, the latest architecture, the infrastructure pieces to plug into.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The thing that really changed is—it used to be you could build a lot, a lot would go straightforward, but then you’d hit some random thing and you could spend an indefinite period of time debugging some narrow thing.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Now, with agents, you just don’t get stuck anymore.
speaker: Max Hodak
